news_summary: Scientists have made a breakthrough in understanding the Amaterasu particle by resolving a longstanding cosmic mystery through timely identification.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont fait une avancée majeure dans la compréhension de la particule Amaterasu en résolvant un mystère cosmique de longue date grâce à une identification opportune.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado un gran avance en la comprensión de la partícula Amaterasu al resolver un antiguo misterio cósmico gracias a su oportuna identificación.

articles.title: Scientists think they solved the mystery of the Amaterasu particle
articles.summary: The mysterious Amaterasu particle may not be a proton at all. New research suggests that some of the most extreme cosmic rays could be ultraheavy atomic nuclei, heavier than iron, which are better able to retain their energy while traveling through space. This idea could help explain how these rare particles reach Earth and provide new clues about the powerful cosmic explosions that create them.
articles.content:
An ultraheavy particle may help explain one of the most puzzling mysteries in modern astrophysics: the origin of the most energetic particles ever detected.

Ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays are particles from space that slam into Earth with energies far beyond anything produced by human-built particle accelerators. Among the most extraordinary examples is the "Amaterasu particle," which was detected by the Telescope Array in Utah in 2021 and named for the sun goddess in Japanese mythology. Its reported energy ranks it among the most powerful cosmic-ray events ever observed, placing it in the same rare category as the "Oh-My-God particle" recorded in 1991. Yet scientists still do not know where it came from, or even exactly what it was.

Ultraheavy Cosmic Rays

New research led by scientists at Penn State and published in Physical Review Letters suggests that some of the highest-energy cosmic rays may be atomic nuclei heavier than iron. Atomic nuclei are the compact centers of atoms, made up of protons and neutrons. They hold almost all of an atom's mass while taking up only a tiny part of its total volume.

According to the team's calculations, these ultraheavy nuclei may lose energy more slowly than protons or lighter nuclei while crossing intergalactic space. That means they could survive the journey to Earth while still carrying extreme amounts of energy. The work, carried out with collaborators at the Yukawa Institute for Theoretical Physics in Japan, Virginia Tech and other institutions, may help scientists identify the types of cosmic objects powerful enough to launch such particles.

"Ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays can only be accelerated by some of the most powerful sources in the universe," said Kohta Murase, professor of physics and of astronomy and astrophysics in the Penn State Eberly College of Science and the leader of the research team. "When we detect individual cosmic-ray particles such as the Amaterasu particle here on Earth, we can often use their energies, arrival directions and expected magnetic deflections to infer their possible cosmic sources."

The Amaterasu Particle Mystery

The Amaterasu particle has been especially difficult to explain because its estimated arrival direction traces back to a cosmic void, a region of space with no clear source capable of producing ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays.

"The origins and acceleration mechanisms of ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays have been among the biggest mysteries in the field for more than 60 years, since the first example was reported," Murase said.

These rare particles can exceed 100 exa-electron volts, or 100 quintillion electron volts. That makes them about seven orders of magnitude, or 10 million times, more energetic than particles accelerated inside the Large Hadron Collider, the world's largest and most powerful particle accelerator. The Amaterasu particle was reported at about 240 exa-electron volts, giving one tiny cosmic-ray particle roughly the kinetic energy of a fast-moving tennis ball. That makes it one of the most energetic cosmic rays ever detected.

"These highest-energy cosmic rays are thought to come from extreme astrophysical sources, like two neutron stars colliding or a massive star collapsing," Murase said. "For many cosmic-ray events taken together, their energy distribution, arrival-direction pattern and statistically inferred composition provide important clues about where these particles come from and how they are accelerated."

Simulating Extreme Particles

To investigate what kinds of particles could still reach Earth at such extraordinary energies, the researchers ran detailed computer simulations. They modeled how particles of different sizes would gain or lose energy while traveling through intergalactic space.

"Our research showed that at energies comparable to that of the Amaterasu particle, ultraheavy nuclei lose energy more slowly than protons or intermediate-mass nuclei, making them better able to survive cosmic distances and reach Earth at extreme energies," Murase said. "We are not saying that all ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays are ultraheavy nuclei. But if some of the highest-energy events are ultraheavy nuclei, that would impact how we search for their sources."

The team's calculations also set new limits on how much these ultraheavy nuclei may contribute to the full population of observed ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays.

Violent Cosmic Origins

"The most promising sites for producing and accelerating such ultraheavy nuclei are massive star deaths involving explosive collapse into black holes or strongly magnetized neutron stars, as well as binary neutron-star mergers known to be powerful gravitational-wave emitters," Murase said. "These violent cosmic phenomena can also power gamma-ray bursts that are among the most energetic explosions in the universe. A contribution from these sources could also help explain a possible difference seen between the northern and southern skies in the ultrahigh-energy cosmic-ray spectrum. If ultraheavy nuclei contribute significantly at the highest energies, future data should indicate a composition heavier than iron."

Future observatories may be able to test these ideas. Murase said next-generation facilities, including the proposed AugerPrime in Argentina and the proposed Global Cosmic Ray Observatory, could look for the predicted signatures. Additional theoretical work on cosmic explosions involving black holes and strongly magnetized neutron stars may also help reveal where ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays are born.

Along with Murase, the research team included B. Theodore Zhang, a postdoctoral researcher at Kyoto University's Yukawa Institute for Theoretical Physics at the time of the research and a former Penn State postdoctoral researcher; Mukul Bhattacharya, an Eberly Postdoctoral Fellow at Penn State at the time of the research; and Nick Ekanger and Shunsaku Horiuchi, who were at Virginia Tech at the time of the research.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in astrophysics, providing new insights into the nature and origin of ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays, including the mysterious Amaterasu particle. This discovery advances understanding of extreme cosmic phenomena with broad implications for space science and astrophysics, offering hope for future research and exploration.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find new clues about the mysterious Amaterasu cosmic particle
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays are particles from space that hit Earth with more energy than anything made on Earth. One famous example is the Amaterasu particle, detected in 2021, which is one of the most energetic particles ever recorded. Scientists have struggled to understand where such particles come from and what they really are.

**What happened** – A team of scientists from Penn State and other institutions studied these cosmic rays and found that some might be ultraheavy atomic nuclei, heavier than iron. These heavy particles lose energy more slowly than lighter ones while traveling through space, allowing them to reach Earth with very high energy. The research used computer simulations to show how these particles behave over long distances.

**Impact** – This idea helps explain how such powerful particles can survive the long journey through space and why the Amaterasu particle came from an empty region of space. It also suggests that violent cosmic events like star explosions and neutron star collisions may create these ultraheavy particles. This new understanding changes how scientists look for the origins of the most energetic cosmic rays.

**What's next step** – Future observatories, like AugerPrime in Argentina and the Global Cosmic Ray Observatory, will test these findings by looking for signs of ultraheavy nuclei in cosmic rays. Additional research on cosmic explosions involving black holes and neutron stars will also help uncover where these particles come from.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists now think some of the most energetic cosmic rays, including the Amaterasu particle, might be ultraheavy atomic nuclei from powerful cosmic explosions, opening new paths to understand their mysterious origins.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent de nouveaux indices sur la mystérieuse particule cosmique Amaterasu
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les rayons cosmiques ultraénergétiques sont des particules venant de l’espace qui frappent la Terre avec plus d’énergie que tout ce que l’on produit sur Terre. Un exemple célèbre est la particule Amaterasu, détectée en 2021, l’une des particules les plus énergétiques jamais enregistrées. Les scientifiques ont eu du mal à comprendre leur origine et leur nature.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs de Penn State et d’autres institutions a découvert que certains de ces rayons cosmiques pourraient être des noyaux atomiques ultra-lourds, plus lourds que le fer. Ces particules lourdes perdent moins d’énergie que les plus légères en voyageant dans l’espace, ce qui leur permet d’arriver sur Terre avec une énergie très élevée. Les chercheurs ont utilisé des simulations informatiques pour étudier leur comportement sur de longues distances.

**Impact** – Cette idée aide à expliquer comment ces particules puissantes peuvent survivre au long voyage dans l’espace et pourquoi la particule Amaterasu vient d’une région vide de l’espace. Elle suggère aussi que des événements cosmiques violents comme des explosions d’étoiles et des collisions d’étoiles à neutrons créent ces particules ultra-lourdes. Cette nouvelle compréhension change la façon dont les scientifiques cherchent l’origine des rayons cosmiques les plus énergétiques.

**Prochaine étape** – De futures observatoires, comme AugerPrime en Argentine et le Global Cosmic Ray Observatory, vont tester ces découvertes en cherchant des signes de noyaux ultra-lourds dans les rayons cosmiques. D’autres recherches sur les explosions cosmiques impliquant des trous noirs et des étoiles à neutrons aideront aussi à découvrir leur origine.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques pensent désormais que certains des rayons cosmiques les plus énergétiques, dont la particule Amaterasu, pourraient être des noyaux atomiques ultra-lourds issus d’explosions cosmiques puissantes, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre leur origine mystérieuse.
articles.personality_title_es: Los científicos encuentran nuevas pistas sobre la misteriosa partícula cósmica Amaterasu
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los rayos cósmicos de ultra alta energía son partículas del espacio que llegan a la Tierra con más energía que cualquier cosa creada en la Tierra. Un ejemplo famoso es la partícula Amaterasu, detectada en 2021, una de las partículas más energéticas jamás registradas. Los científicos han tenido dificultades para entender de dónde vienen y qué son exactamente.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de científicos de Penn State y otras instituciones descubrió que algunos de estos rayos cósmicos podrían ser núcleos atómicos ultra pesados, más pesados que el hierro. Estas partículas pesadas pierden energía más lentamente que las más ligeras al viajar por el espacio, permitiéndoles llegar a la Tierra con mucha energía. Los investigadores usaron simulaciones por computadora para estudiar cómo se comportan estas partículas a grandes distancias.

**Impacto** – Esta idea ayuda a explicar cómo estas partículas tan poderosas pueden sobrevivir el largo viaje por el espacio y por qué la partícula Amaterasu llegó desde una región vacía del espacio. También sugiere que eventos cósmicos violentos como explosiones de estrellas y colisiones de estrellas de neutrones podrían crear estas partículas ultra pesadas. Este nuevo entendimiento cambia cómo los científicos buscan el origen de los rayos cósmicos más energéticos.

**Próximo paso** – Observatorios futuros, como AugerPrime en Argentina y el Observatorio Global de Rayos Cósmicos, probarán estas ideas buscando señales de núcleos ultra pesados en los rayos cósmicos. Más investigaciones sobre explosiones cósmicas con agujeros negros y estrellas de neutrones también ayudarán a descubrir de dónde vienen estas partículas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos creen que algunas de las partículas cósmicas más energéticas, incluida la partícula Amaterasu, podrían ser núcleos atómicos ultra pesados de explosiones cósmicas poderosas, abriendo nuevas formas de entender su origen misterioso.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a radiant, ultraheavy atomic nucleus glowing with warm golden and deep amber hues, soaring through a vast cosmic void filled with faint stars and subtle intergalactic clouds, symbolizing its extraordinary energy and mysterious journey from distant violent cosmic explosions toward Earth’s atmosphere.

articles.title: A cosmic case of mistaken identity that can only be solved right now
articles.summary: Brown dwarfs are somewhere between the size of a planet and a star, so how could we have potentially mistaken two of them for distant galaxies? Columnist Chanda Prescod-Weinstein argues that solving this cosmic mix-up is particularly possible now, as galaxy research has never been stronger
articles.content:
Brown dwarfs are somewhere between the size of a planet and a star, so how could we have potentially mistaken two of them for distant galaxies? Columnist Chanda Prescod-Weinstein argues that solving this cosmic mix-up is particularly possible now, as galaxy research has never been stronger

The galaxy MoM-z14 was called a “cosmic miracle” by its discoverers J. DePasquale (STScI)

I’m certain scientists before me have thought this about their own era, but nonetheless, I think we are living in a wildly exciting time for galaxy research. Though US and UK research budgets might be doing the opposite, our understanding of the universe is rapidly expanding. In just the few years since its launch on Christmas 2021, the James Webb Space Telescope (what I prefer to call the Just Wonderful Space Telescope, or JWST) has transformed our understanding of what galaxies looked like in the early universe. It turns out they were brighter – more full of stars and hot gas – than our standard cosmological picture had previously accounted for.

These observations by JWST are incredibly exciting. We are seeing galaxies that existed only 300 million years after the big bang. Of course, this sounds like a very long time. But compared to the age of the universe – which is a little under 14 billion years old – 300 million is the universe’s baby era. I’m 43, and the same percentage (2.14 per cent) of my life is when I was less than a year old.

Rohan P. Naidu at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology led a global team of researchers that discovered one such galaxy last year and dubbed it MoM-z14. The team’s paper refers to MoM as a “cosmic miracle” – brighter than we might have expected a galaxy could possibly be at such an early stage in the evolution of the cosmos. The reason observations like this are only happening for the first time is that previously we didn’t have a telescope with the ability to see objects that were so faint – given how far they are from us. It feels a little bit weird to say that this very faint thing is unexpectedly bright, but that’s the business of astronomy. It is extremely dim for a normal telescope, but compared to theoretical predictions, it is emitting more light than expected.

While the luminous nature of these galaxies makes them scientifically tantalising, their absolute faintness makes them a kind of technical challenge. We must ask ourselves whether we are certain the object being imaged is in fact a galaxy. This past April, Maruša Bradač at the University of California, Davis and a global team of collaborators dug into the data and concluded that two of JWST’s young bright galaxies were not galaxies at all! The objects, the team claims in a paper that has not yet been peer-reviewed, are actually brown dwarfs in the Milky Way: star-ish objects inside our own galaxy, rather than galaxies that are far, far away.

I was completely taken aback when I saw this paper because if you asked me what’s the opposite of a galaxy, a brown dwarf would be a good candidate. Too big to be a gas-giant planet like Jupiter, but too small to sustain stellar fusion – a brown dwarf is something that could plausibly fit into a solar system. In other words, brown dwarfs are small and compact compared with the expanse that is a galaxy filled with billions of stars. Only time will tell whether Bradač’s team is right and whether other old galaxy observations are actually a case of mistaken identity.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

But we’re well-equipped to find out. My prediction is that 2026 will be remembered as a landmark year for astronomy. Despite attempts by governments to crush research, this year the Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile will begin its ten-year Legacy Survey of Space and Time, which will create a detailed map of the southern-hemisphere sky and images of over five billion galaxies. Rubin will not only operate simultaneously with JWST, but also with NASA’s next flagship observatory, the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope. Roman, which complements the European Space Agency’s currently flying Euclid mission, will image hundreds of millions of galaxies. Together, JWST, Rubin, Roman and Euclid may change everything we know about how a place like our home galaxy, the Milky Way, came to exist.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant advancement in astronomy enabled by the James Webb Space Telescope and upcoming observatories, which is expanding our understanding of the early universe and galaxy formation. It discusses a scientific challenge and potential correction in identifying cosmic objects, emphasizing ongoing and future research that promises to transform knowledge about the cosmos. This represents a meaningful, inspiring breakthrough in space science with broad implications for humanity's understanding of the universe.
articles.personality_title: New telescopes help solve cosmic mix-up between stars and galaxies
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Brown dwarfs are objects in space that are bigger than planets but smaller than stars. Recently, some of these brown dwarfs were mistaken for very distant galaxies by scientists using a powerful new telescope.

**What happened** – In 2021, the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) started sending back images of the early universe. It showed galaxies that existed just 300 million years after the Big Bang, which is very early in cosmic history. Scientists were excited to find a galaxy called MoM-z14, which was brighter than expected for its age. But in April 2023, a team led by Maruša Bradač found that two of these bright objects were not galaxies at all, but brown dwarfs inside our own Milky Way.

**Impact** – This mix-up shows how challenging it is to study very faint and distant objects in space. It also highlights how new technology like JWST can change what we know about the universe. Discovering that some objects thought to be distant galaxies are actually nearby brown dwarfs helps scientists improve their methods and avoid mistakes in the future.

**What's next step** – Starting in 2026, new telescopes like the Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile and NASA's Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope will work alongside JWST. Together, they will study billions of galaxies and create detailed maps of the sky. These projects will help scientists better understand how galaxies, including our own Milky Way, formed and evolved.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New powerful telescopes are revealing surprising details about the universe and correcting earlier mistakes, paving the way for deeper discoveries about galaxies and stars.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux télescopes corrigent une confusion cosmique entre étoiles et galaxies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les naines brunes sont des objets dans l’espace plus gros que des planètes mais plus petits que des étoiles. Récemment, certaines de ces naines brunes ont été confondues avec des galaxies très lointaines par des scientifiques utilisant un nouveau télescope puissant.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2021, le télescope spatial James Webb (JWST) a commencé à envoyer des images de l’univers primitif. Il a montré des galaxies qui existaient seulement 300 millions d’années après le Big Bang, ce qui est très tôt dans l’histoire cosmique. Les scientifiques étaient enthousiastes de découvrir une galaxie appelée MoM-z14, plus brillante que prévu pour son âge. Mais en avril 2023, une équipe menée par Maruša Bradač a découvert que deux de ces objets lumineux n’étaient pas des galaxies, mais des naines brunes situées dans notre propre Voie lactée.

**Impact** – Cette confusion montre à quel point il est difficile d’étudier des objets très faibles et lointains dans l’espace. Elle souligne aussi comment la nouvelle technologie comme JWST peut changer notre compréhension de l’univers. Découvrir que certains objets pensés être des galaxies lointaines sont en fait des naines brunes proches aide les scientifiques à améliorer leurs méthodes et éviter des erreurs à l’avenir.

**Prochaine étape** – À partir de 2026, de nouveaux télescopes comme l’Observatoire Vera C. Rubin au Chili et le télescope spatial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA travailleront avec JWST. Ensemble, ils étudieront des milliards de galaxies et créeront des cartes détaillées du ciel. Ces projets aideront les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment les galaxies, y compris notre Voie lactée, se sont formées et ont évolué.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouveaux télescopes puissants révèlent des détails surprenants sur l’univers et corrigent des erreurs passées, ouvrant la voie à des découvertes plus profondes sur les galaxies et les étoiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos telescopios corrigen confusión cósmica entre estrellas y galaxias
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las enanas marrones son objetos en el espacio que son más grandes que los planetas pero más pequeñas que las estrellas. Recientemente, algunas de estas enanas marrones fueron confundidas con galaxias muy lejanas por científicos que usaban un telescopio nuevo y muy poderoso.

**Qué pasó** – En 2021, el telescopio espacial James Webb (JWST) empezó a enviar imágenes del universo temprano. Mostró galaxias que existieron solo 300 millones de años después del Big Bang, que es muy temprano en la historia cósmica. Los científicos se emocionaron al encontrar una galaxia llamada MoM-z14, que era más brillante de lo esperado para su edad. Pero en abril de 2023, un equipo liderado por Maruša Bradač descubrió que dos de estos objetos brillantes no eran galaxias, sino enanas marrones dentro de nuestra propia Vía Láctea.

**Impacto** – Esta confusión muestra lo difícil que es estudiar objetos muy tenues y distantes en el espacio. También demuestra cómo la nueva tecnología como JWST puede cambiar lo que sabemos sobre el universo. Descubrir que algunos objetos que se creían galaxias lejanas son en realidad enanas marrones cercanas ayuda a los científicos a mejorar sus métodos y evitar errores en el futuro.

**Próximo paso** – A partir de 2026, nuevos telescopios como el Observatorio Vera C. Rubin en Chile y el Telescopio Espacial Nancy Grace Roman de la NASA trabajarán junto con JWST. Juntos estudiarán miles de millones de galaxias y crearán mapas detallados del cielo. Estos proyectos ayudarán a los científicos a entender mejor cómo se formaron y evolucionaron las galaxias, incluida nuestra propia Vía Láctea.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Nuevos telescopios poderosos están revelando detalles sorprendentes del universo y corrigiendo errores anteriores, abriendo el camino para descubrimientos más profundos sobre galaxias y estrellas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast cosmic scene where two glowing, small brown dwarfs—depicted as softly radiant amber orbs—are gently mistaken for distant, shimmering galaxies made of bright star clusters and swirling hot gas, set against a deep, dark space background illuminated by the subtle glow of early-universe galaxies, evoking a sense of discovery and the unfolding mystery of the cosmos.
